elements.sent-2411-001: We introduce DiHuR, a novel Diffusion-guided model for generalizable Human 3D Reconstruction and view synthesis from sparse, minimally overlapping images.
elements.sent-2411-002: While existing generalizable human radiance fields excel at novel view synthesis, they often struggle with comprehensive 3D reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-003: Similarly, directly optimizing implicit Signed Distance Function (SDF) fields from sparse-view images typically yields poor results due to limited overlap.
elements.sent-2411-004: To enhance 3D reconstruction quality, we propose using learnable tokens associated with SMPL vertices to aggregate sparse view features and then to guide SDF prediction.
elements.sent-2411-005: These tokens learn a generalizable prior across different identities in training datasets, leveraging the consistent projection of SMPL vertices onto similar semantic areas across various human identities.
elements.sent-2411-006: This consistency enables effective knowledge transfer to unseen identities during inference.
elements.sent-2411-007: Recognizing SMPL's limitations in capturing clothing details, we incorporate a diffusion model as an additional prior to fill in missing information, particularly for complex clothing geometries.
elements.sent-2411-008: Our method integrates two key priors in a coherent manner: the prior from generalizable feed-forward models and the 2D diffusion prior, and it requires only multi-view image training, without 3D supervision.
elements.sent-2411-009: DiHuR demonstrates superior performance in both within-dataset and cross-dataset generalization settings, as validated on THuman, ZJU-MoCap, and HuMMan datasets compared to existing methods.
elements.sent-2411-010: Neural scene representation\cite{nerf20 enables the realistic generation of 3D digital models from 2D observations of the scene that are useful for many real-world applications such as Augmented and Virtual Reality (AR/VR), and the creation of digital avatars.}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.48\textwidth]{teaser.pdf} \caption{Given 3 views of images with minimal overlap FOV, our method can accurately reconstruct 3D human models.} \label{fig:teaser}
elements.sent-2411-011: Although existing {generalizable human NeRF \cite{nb21,anerf21,gpnerf22,mps22,arah22,sherf23 have achieved impressive, they mainly focus on novel view synthesis.}}
elements.sent-2411-012: The surfaces extracted from these fields are often unsatisfactory, particularly in sparse view settings.
elements.sent-2411-013: This is due to the little or non-existent overlap between views, which complicates feature aggregation.
elements.sent-2411-014: In such scenarios, it becomes challenging to identify the correct features with low variance(high confidence) across views.
elements.sent-2411-015: Consequently, the algorithm often resorts to simple averaging, leading to suboptimal results in surface reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-016: In this paper, we focus on the challenging task of generalizable 3D human reconstruction and NVS with images from sparse cameras.
elements.sent-2411-017: To this end, {we propose the Diffusion-guided Generalizable Human NeRF (DiHuR) framework , which leverages the SMPL model~\cite{smpl15 to build geometric-consistent features to infer the 3D structure and incorporates pre-trained diffusion models as geometric guidance to enhance reconstruction quality.}}
elements.sent-2411-018: Specifically, we fuse the feature by cross-view attention with the learnable tokens attached on the SMPL vertices.
elements.sent-2411-019: The fused features are further refined with several self-attention layers among all the tokens for information exchange.
elements.sent-2411-020: In order to sample the sparse features attached on the fixed number of SMPL vertices, we compute K nearest neighbours and average K features based on the distances.
elements.sent-2411-021: Although effective, the SMPL model is learned from minimal clothes body data and thus fails to model geometric details on the clothes.
elements.sent-2411-022: To solve this, we render the normal map from several target views and feed them to the pre-trained 2D diffusion model to compute SDS loss for fast finetuning.
elements.sent-2411-023: We adapt the diffusion model \cite{diffusion22 for super-resolution to provide detailed 3D geometry prior.}
elements.sent-2411-024: We first render the normal map from our model, which is then upsampled 4x and used as the input of the diffusion model.
elements.sent-2411-025: The rendered map is also used together with the text as the condition for the denoising process.
elements.sent-2411-026: SDS loss is back-propagated to update our model parameters.
elements.sent-2411-027: A multi-target optimization strategy is also proposed to implicitly regularize the 3D surface.
elements.sent-2411-028: We simultaneously sample rays from different camera views, focusing on the same body part.
elements.sent-2411-029: This ensures intersecting rays between views, which implicitly enforce multi-view consistency during surface prediction.
elements.sent-2411-030: We also apply a second-order Signed Distance Function (SDF) regularization to enhance surface smoothness.
elements.sent-2411-031: Fig~\ref{fig:teaser shows examples of our accurate 3D reconstruction results on the sparse camera setting.}
elements.sent-2411-032: Our main contributions can be summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-033: We propose DiHuR to solve the challenging task of generalizable 3D human reconstruction and NVS in the sparse views.
elements.sent-2411-034: We propose to use learnable latent codes attached on SMPL vertices to guide the sparse view reconstruction and 2D prior from the diffusion model to enhance the details.
elements.sent-2411-035: We achieve SOTA-performance for 3D human reconstruction, and novel view synthesis on the commonly used dataset in the sparse view settings.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=1\textwidth]{jn_framework.pdf} \caption{The overview of our proposed DiHuR. Our pipeline mainly consists of three parts: 1) Feature aggregation, 2) SDF prediction, and 3) Appearance prediction. Our learnable tokens serve as query tokens to aggregate sparse view features, which is then interpolated by KNN for each query point. Volume rendering is done with aggregated features and mean, variance of the projected features from sparse input views. During inference, we finetune our model with normal SDS loss to enhance the details.} \label{fig:framework}
elements.sent-2411-036: NB~\cite{nb21 first combines NeRF with a parametric human body model SMPL \cite{smpl15 to regularize the optimization process.}}
elements.sent-2411-037: Although it achieves surprising NVS results, it needs long-term optimization and is hard to generalize to unseen identities.
elements.sent-2411-038: Ani-NeRF \cite{animatable21 learns a canonical NeRF model and a backward LBS network which predicts residuals to the deterministic SMPL-based backward LBS (Linear Blending Skinning) to animate the learned human NeRF model.}
elements.sent-2411-039: A-NeRF \cite{anerf21 designs a backward mapping with bone-relative representation for feature encoding, which also helps the initial pose correction.}
elements.sent-2411-040: Subsequently, generalizable NeRFs are \cite{ibrnet21,mvsnerf21,pixelnerf20,sherf23 are proposed to make NeRF generalizable to novel scenes in a feed-forward way.}
elements.sent-2411-041: They commonly condition the NeRF on the pixel-aligned image features and learn blending weights or directly infer the RGB color condition on such features.
elements.sent-2411-042: However, due to the non-rigid structure and complicated poses of the human body, directly applying such methods fails to work well on real human datasets.
elements.sent-2411-043: However, the 3D reconstruction is far from satisfying due to the lack of 3D constraints, and the NVS is blurry without awareness of the 3D geometry.
elements.sent-2411-044: More recently, people combine 3D gaussian splatting with human template model to reconstruct high quality humans \cite{chen2024generalizablehumangaussianssingleview,hu2023gauhumanarticulatedgaussiansplatting,liu2023humangaussian .}
elements.sent-2411-045: However, accurate surface extraction from noisy 3DGS remains a chanllenge.
elements.sent-2411-046: PIFu\cite{pifu19 is first proposed to reconstruct 3D model from single image with high quality.}
elements.sent-2411-047: Subsequently, PIFuHD\cite{pifuhd20 and ECON\cite{econ23 is proposed to improve geometric quality with multi-level pixel-aligned features and normal features as additional guidance.}}
elements.sent-2411-048: More recently, SiTH \cite{sith24 proposes to combine a back-view hallucination model with an SDF-based mesh reconstruction model.}
elements.sent-2411-049: Similarly, SiFU\cite{sifu24 employs a text-to-image diffusion-based prior to generate realistic and consistent textures for invisible views.}
elements.sent-2411-050: However, these methods focus on single image reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-051: Although these methods attempt to hallucinate reasonable interpretations of these invisible areas, the generated regions may not always align with users’ expectations.
elements.sent-2411-052: Also, ground truth 3D supervision are used for all of these methods.
elements.sent-2411-053: Compared to these methods, our approach can be trained solely on multi-view images and achieves better performance on sparse view settings.
elements.sent-2411-054: Recently, diffusion models \cite{diffusion22 have dominated the image generation area for its exceptional generative quality.}
elements.sent-2411-055: Specifically, they introduce a Score Distillation Sampling (SDS) loss function, which formulates the same loss function used in the original diffusion model training process, but back-propagate the gradients towards the input rendered images and keeps the pre-trained diffusion models as fixed.
elements.sent-2411-056: With only this SDS loss as supervision, they can generate fantastic 3D meshes with text as an input condition.
elements.sent-2411-057: However, they often require lengthy optimization due to SDS loss computation and per-scene NeRF optimization from scratch.
elements.sent-2411-058: Inspired by these developments, our approach combines the SDS loss with an initial reconstruction from a generalizable NeRF model.
elements.sent-2411-059: This strategy preserves the prior information from pre-trained diffusion models and leverages the efficiency of feed-forward NeRF models, reducing overall optimization time.
elements.sent-2411-060: We propose a generalizable reconstruction approach for 3D human reconstruction with sparse cameras.
elements.sent-2411-061: The key challenge in this generalizable setting is how to fuse the features extracted from multi-view images with minimal overlaps, \eg 3 cameras placed $120^\circ$ apart around the subject.
elements.sent-2411-062: A naive aggregation of the multi-view image features leads to high variance due to inconsistent features from self-occlusions.
elements.sent-2411-063: As a result, the network cannot differentiate between the self-occluded points and points in the free space.
elements.sent-2411-064: Without any supervision on the blending weights, the network can assign colors of occluded image pixels with high blending weights leading to inaccurate color prediction.
elements.sent-2411-065: To circumvent this problem, we propose to set learnable tokens anchored on the SMPL vertices which can learn the common prior across the training dataset to guide the SDF prediction.
elements.sent-2411-066: After the direct inference, we render the normal map from multi-views and finetune the partial parameters with normal SDS loss from the 2D pre-trained diffusion model as geometric guidance to enhance the details.
elements.sent-2411-067: As illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:framework , our DiHuR is a volume rendering pipeline with SDF-based volume density representation.}
elements.sent-2411-068: We first compute features for each sample point from two sources, \ie directly 3D-to-2D projected features' mean and variance, as well as sparse features aggregated from learnable tokens.
elements.sent-2411-069: These two features are concatenated and then used to predict SDF value along the ray and a geometry code that represents geometry embedding.
elements.sent-2411-070: For color prediction, use the geometry code and relative ray direction from each of the source camera centers for blending weights prediction.
elements.sent-2411-071: We use ground truth RGB images as supervision.
elements.sent-2411-072: During training, we use multiple target images simultaneously as the supervision to better regularize the underlying 3D geometry.
elements.sent-2411-073: During inference, we finetune our partial model parameters with normal SDS loss.
elements.sent-2411-074: Given $S$ source images $\{ I_{s \}_{s=1 ^{S}$ of a human captured by $S$ sparse cameras with minimal overlap FOV, we first use a multi-resolution image feature extractor to get multi-view features $\{F_{s \}_{s=1 ^{S}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-075: We use Plucker ray embedding to densely encode the camera poses.
elements.sent-2411-076: The collection of the RGB value and ray embedding for each pixel are concatenated into a 9-channel feature map as the input \(I=\{c_{i ,o_{i \times d_{i ,d_{i \mid i=1,2,...,N\}\), where i is the pixel index.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-077: In order to aggregate the accurate features from sparse views and make the feature occlusion-aware, we set learnable tokens attached on each of the SMPL vertices as $\{T_{q \mid q=1, 2, ..., 6890\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-078: Subsequently, we aggregate the features from $S$ source views for each vertex $p_{q}$ with a multi-head cross-view attention module to obtain the SMPL aggregated feature $F_{q ^{a}$ as: \begin{equation} \begin{aligned} F_{q}^{a}=\sum_{s=1}^{S} w_{s}^{q}F_{s}(p_{q}) \end{aligned} \label{eq:smpl_sum}\end{equation} \begin{equation} \begin{aligned} w_{s}^{q} = SoftMax(\frac{(L_{q}(T_{q} )(L_{k}(F_{s}(p_{q}))^\top}{\sqrt{d}}). \end{aligned} \label{eq:smpl_cross}\end{equation} ,where $L_{q}$ $L_{k}$ are MLP for query and key embedding, $d$ is the model dimensions.}
elements.sent-2411-079: Then, we add self-attention layers to let all the tokens' feature attend each other.
elements.sent-2411-080: Our approach leverages learnable tokens associated with SMPL vertices.
elements.sent-2411-081: These tokens acquire a generalizable prior across diverse identities during training and exploit the consistent mapping of SMPL vertices to similar semantic regions on different human bodies, which serves as a human prior facilitating knowledge transfer to novel identities during inference.
elements.sent-2411-082: The SMPL feature is sparse in the 3D space since it only contains $6,890$ vertices.
elements.sent-2411-083: When we sample each point on the ray, we first identify K-Nearest Neighbours from all the SMPL vertices and compute the aggregated features based on the distance. \begin{equation} \begin{aligned} Fus(p)= \sum_{k=1}^{K} w_{k}F_{k}^{a} \end{aligned} \label{eq:smpl}\end{equation} where $w_{k}$ is computed with an inverse distance function and softmax function.
elements.sent-2411-084: To infer the SDF value for a 3D point $p$, we project the 3D point to the source image spaces and compute the mean and variance of the image features, \ie $\mu=\frac{1 {S}\sum_{s=1 ^{S F_{s (p)$ and $v=$Var$(\{ F_{s (p)\}_{s=1 ^{S )$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-085: We denote the concatenation of the mean and variance as the global feature $F^{glo (p)= [ \mu,v ]$.}
elements.sent-2411-086: The global feature is then concatenated with the KNN fused feature at $p$.
elements.sent-2411-087: The concatenated feature is fed into the SDF prediction network $\mathcal F^{s }$ to predict the SDF value: \begin{equation} \begin{aligned} \{s(p),Z^{geo}\}=\mathcal F^{s }(\gamma(p), [F^{glo}(p) ,Fus(p)] ), \end{aligned} \label{eq:SDF }\end{equation} where $\gamma$ denotes the positional encoding, and $s(p)$ denotes the predicted SDF value at $p$.
elements.sent-2411-088: The network also outputs a geometry code $Z^{geo}$ which can be seen as an implicit local 3D structure encoding.
elements.sent-2411-089: This geometry code is used for the color prediction in the next step.
elements.sent-2411-090: We first blend the color among sparse views and then integrate the blended color along the ray to predict the final pixel color.
elements.sent-2411-091: We first compute the difference between two views $d$ and $d_{s}$ as $\Delta d_{s =d-d_{s}$.}
elements.sent-2411-092: We encode this vector to a high dimension as $Enc(\Delta d)$.
elements.sent-2411-093: We then add the view direction feature to the original image features to obtain a new image feature expressed as: \begin{equation} \begin{aligned} F^{\oplus}_{s}=F_{s}+Enc(\Delta d). \end{aligned} \label{eq:raydiff}\end{equation} Then, we concatenate image feature $F^{\oplus _{s}$, the geometry code $Z^{geo}$ and feed it into our blending weights prediction network $\mathcal F^{w}$ for view blending weights $w_{s}$ prediction: \begin{equation} \begin{aligned} w_{s}=\mathcal F^{w}(F^{\oplus}_{s},Z^{geo}). \end{aligned} \label{eq:blendweights}\end{equation} This information contains rich geometry clues to help the network infer the correct blending weight for the source images with few color correspondences due to view sparsity.}
elements.sent-2411-094: The color for each 3D point is the weighted sum of the projected color $c_{s = I_{s (\pi_{s (p) $ from $S$ source images, \ie: \begin{equation} \begin{aligned} c=\sum_{s=1}^{S} w_{s}c_{s}. \end{aligned} \label{eq:blend}\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-095: For volume rendering along the rays, we follow the NeuS \cite{neus21 formulation to convert the SDF value $F^{s (p)$ to the density $\sigma$ , \ie: \begin{equation} \begin{aligned} \sigma=max(-\frac{ \frac{d\Phi }{dp} (\mathcal F^{s}(p) ) }{ \Phi (\mathcal F^{s }(p) )},0), \end{aligned} \label{eq:neus}\end{equation} where $p$ is the point sampled on the ray and $\Phi$ is the Sigmoid function.}}
elements.sent-2411-096: Finally, for a ray sampled with M points, the color is accumulated as: \begin{equation} \begin{aligned} C(r)=\sum_{i=1}^{M}T_{i}(1-exp(-\sigma_{i}))_{i}, \text{where} ~~ T_{i}=exp(-\sum_{j=1}^{i-1} \sigma_{j}). \end{aligned} \label{eq:volume}\end{equation}.
elements.subsection-2411-004: Score Distillation Sampling for Geometry Enhancement
elements.sent-2411-097: During the inference, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:sds , we compute the $\mathcal{L _{sds}$ \cite{dream23 with an pre-trained diffusion model.}}}
elements.sent-2411-098: Different from the original SDS loss in \cite{dream23 , we choose to use a normal map as the input to the super-resolution diffusion model in order to enhance the geometric details.}
elements.sent-2411-099: More specifically, we use our pretrained model to infer the normal map as the low-resolution image condition.
elements.sent-2411-100: We then fine-tune our model with the guidance from the diffusion model.
elements.sent-2411-101: Note that we only finetune $\mathcal{F _{s}$ for fast convergence.}
elements.sent-2411-102: We rendered the normal map and up-sample 4$\times$ for this normal map and subsequently get the latent code from the VAE encoder in the diffusion model.
elements.sent-2411-103: The normal $N(r)$ for each ray is computed by volume rendering with the gradient of the points' SDF sampled on each ray as: \begin{equation} \begin{aligned} N(r)=\sum_{i=1}^{M}T_{i}(1-exp(-\sigma_{i})) \frac{\nabla \mathcal{S}(p_{i})}{||\nabla \mathcal{S}(p_{i})||}, \text{where} ~~ T_{i}=exp(-\sum_{j=1}^{i-1} \sigma_{j}), \end{aligned} \label{eq:normal}\end{equation} We finally compute the SDS loss: \begin{equation} \begin{aligned} \mathcal{L}_{sds}=w(t)(\hat{\epsilon}_{\phi}(z_{t} ;y,N^{low},t)-\epsilon) \frac{\partial z_{t}}{\partial N} \frac{\partial N}{\partial \theta}, \end{aligned} \label{eq:sds}\end{equation} with the input text and low-resolution normal map rendered from our fixed model as conditions.
elements.sent-2411-104: For classifier-free guidance(CFG), we set the guidance weights as 7.5.
elements.sent-2411-105: The intuition is to provide detailed normal supervision for our pre-trained network based on the 2D image super-resolution diffusion model.
elements.sent-2411-106: During SDS refinement, we select 8 views with azimuth evenly increasing from 0 to 360 degree to render the normal map.
elements.sent-2411-107: We also add image reconstruction loss for the input views as well as other regularization losses.
elements.sent-2411-108: The whole process takes 150 iterations around 2 minutes.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=0.46\textwidth]{sds.pdf} \caption{We show how to compute SDS loss on the pre-trained super-resolution diffusion model: the low-resolution conditioned image on the right is generated from our fixed model.} \label{fig:sds}
elements.sent-2411-109: We believe multi-view images can serve as the surrogate for 3D supervision when direct supervision of the SDF values is not available.
elements.sent-2411-110: Consequently, we use multiple images from different views as the target in each iteration instead of using one.
elements.sent-2411-111: The random sampling strategy used in the original NeRF can lead to non-intersecting rays between the multiple target views.
elements.sent-2411-112: To solve this problem, we sample patches from the same body part in each target image to concentrate the optimization zone.
elements.sent-2411-113: Specifically, we simultaneously sample patches from the same segmentation part, \eg upper body, in the multiple target images at each iteration.
elements.sent-2411-114: This sampling strategy results in more overlapping points from different views, hence providing more explicit multi-view supervision.
elements.sent-2411-115: The loss of our multi-target optimization is given by: \begin{equation} \begin{aligned} \mathcal{L}_{rgb}=\sum_{l=1}^{L}\sum_{r\in R} ||\hat C(r)_{l}-C(r)_{l} ||. \end{aligned} \label{eq:mul}\end{equation} $L$ is the number of target images for ray sampling and $R$ is the set of sampled rays for each target image.
elements.sent-2411-116: We enforce the normal vectors of nearby points to be similar with a smooth regularization, \ie : \begin{equation} \begin{aligned} \mathcal{L}_{sm}=\sum_{p \in S }|| \nabla \mathcal S(p) -\nabla \mathcal S(p+ \epsilon ) ||_{2}^{2}, \end{aligned} \label{eq:sm}\end{equation} where $S$ represents the set of sampled points, and $\epsilon$ represents a perturbation sampled from a Gaussian distribution.
elements.sent-2411-117: We also include Eikonal regularization, \ie : \begin{equation} \begin{aligned} \mathcal L_{eik}= \sum_{p \in S}(||\nabla \mathcal F^{s }(p) ||_{2}-1)^{2}. \end{aligned} \label{eq:eik}\end{equation} Our full objective function contains the color loss $\mathcal{L _{rgb}$, SDS loss, the Eikonal regularization \cite{eik20 $\mathcal{L _{eik}$, second-order SDF regularization $\mathcal{L _{sm}$ with corresponding weights as hyper-parameters, \ie: \begin{equation} \begin{aligned} \mathcal{L}=\lambda_{rgb}\mathcal{L}_{rgb}+\lambda_{sds}\mathcal{L}_{sds}+\lambda_{eik}\mathcal{L}_{eik}+\lambda_{sm}\mathcal{L}_{sm}. \end{aligned} \label{eq:loss}\end{equation} Note that only during the per-scene finetuning stage, we add SDS loss.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-118: We conduct experiments on the commonly used ZJU-MoCap dataset \cite{nb21 , THuman dataset \cite{thuman19 and HuMMan dataset\cite{ntuhuanman22 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-120: Following \cite{gpnerf22 , $80\%$ of the scans are taken as the training set, and the remaining are the test set.}
elements.sent-2411-121: For ZJU-MoCap dataset, it consists of 9 sequences captured with 23 calibrated cameras.
elements.sent-2411-122: Following \cite{gpnerf22 , we train our model using 6 sequences and test our model on the remaining 3 sequences for 3D reconstruction.}
elements.sent-2411-123: We further train our method on THuman dataset and conduct cross-dataset evaluation on HuMMan\cite{ntuhuanman22 dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-124: Specifically, we use the last 22 sequences for evaluation.
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=0.9\textwidth]{3dvis.pdf} \caption{ From left to right are reconstruction results from NeuS\cite{neus21}, PIFuHD\cite{pifuhd20}, SparseNeuS\cite{sparseneus22}, GP-NeRF\cite{gpnerf22}, SiTH\cite{sith24}, SIFU\cite{sifu24}, Ours, and ground truth. For single view reconstruction methods\cite{pifuhd20,sith24,sifu24}, we choose the front view as input.} \label{fig:3dvis}
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=0.5\textwidth]{thuman3d.pdf} \caption{Visual comparison on THuman dataset. From left to right are results from SparseNeuS\cite{sparseneus22}, MPS-NeRF\cite{mps22}, GP-NeRF\cite{gpnerf22}, SiTH\cite{sith24}, SIFU\cite{sifu24}, Ours and ground truth.} \label{fig:thu3dvis}
elements.sent-2411-125: Baselines: we compare our method with Generalizable NeRF methods: MPS-NeRF \cite{mps22 , SparseNeuS\cite{sparseneus22 and GP-NeRF \cite{gpnerf22 as well as single view human reconstruction methods PIFuHD\cite{pifuhd20 , SiTH\cite{sith24 and SIFU \cite{sifu24 .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-126: On ZJU-MoCap dataset, we also compared with NeuS\cite{neus21 and NB\cite{nb21 .}}
elements.sent-2411-127: Following \cite{gpnerf22 , we use 3 source views with minimal overlap FOV as the input.}
elements.sent-2411-128: For single view reconstuction methods, we choose the front view as input.
elements.sent-2411-129: We use commonly used Chamfer Distance (CD) and Normal Consistency (NC) as the metrics for evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-130: We show our 3D reconstruction results with other existing methods in Tab.
elements.sent-2411-131: All the NeRF-based models are trained and tested on the same splits without 3D supervision.
elements.sent-2411-132: Our methods outperform existing methods \cite{gpnerf22,sparseneus22,neus21,nb21,mps22,sifu24 with a large margin in terms of both CD and NC.}
elements.sent-2411-133: We show the qualitative comparison with existing methods in Fig.~\ref{fig:3dvis .}
elements.sent-2411-134: We can see in the figure that genralizable NeRF methods\cite{gpnerf22,mps22,sparseneus22 fail to reconstruct the details although the pose and shape are correct.}
elements.sent-2411-135: Directly optimizing NeuS in this setting produces large noisy areas with disconnected points as shown in Fig. \ref{fig:3dvis .}
elements.sent-2411-136: PIFuHD also shows poor generalization ability on this dataset with missing arms and legs as shown in Fig. \ref{fig:3dvis with extremely large error, and therefore we do not include it in the quantitative results.}
elements.sent-2411-137: SiTH and SIFU tend to reconstruct smooth meshes with wrong poses due to single view ambiguity, e.g. the second row in Fig. \ref{fig:3dvis and Fig. \ref{fig:thu3dvis compared with GT.}}
elements.sent-2411-138: In contrast, our model can infer a more realistic human mesh with fine details such as wrinkles on the clothes as well as correct body shape and pose.
elements.sent-2411-139: We also show cross-dataset results in Fig.\ref{fig:wild to demonstrate the generalization ability, where our model is trained on ZJU-MoCap dataset.}
elements.figure-2411-007: \setlength{\tabcolsep}{0.2cm} \begin{tabular}{c|c|c|c|c|c|c|} \multicolumn{1}{c|}{Methods} {\cite{sparseneus22}} {\cite{mps22}} {\cite{gpnerf22}} \cite{sith24} \cite{sifu24} {Ours} CD($\downarrow$) 2.312 3.312 3.876 1.621 1.521 1.117 NC($\uparrow$) 0.634 0.616 0.567 0.723 0.741 0.779 \end{tabular} \caption{3D reconstruction comparison on THuman\cite{thuman19} dataset.} \label{table:thuman3D}
elements.figure-2411-008: \setlength{\tabcolsep}{0.2cm} \begin{tabular}{c|c|c|c|c|c|c|} \multicolumn{1}{c|}{Methods} {\cite{sparseneus22}} {\cite{mps22}} {\cite{gpnerf22}} \cite{sith24} \cite{sifu24} {Ours} CD($\downarrow$) 3.211 3.412 3.765 1.723 1.709 1.23 NC($\uparrow$) 0.633 0.621 0.612 0.711 0.721 0.753 \end{tabular} \caption{Cross-dataset evaluation on HuMMan\cite{ntuhuanman22} dataset.} \label{table:human3D}

elements.figure-2411-010: \includegraphics[width=1.0\textwidth]{2dvis.pdf} \caption{Visual comparison with GP-NeRF \cite{gpnerf22}. From left to right are the results from: 1) Seen body with seen pose; 2) Seen body with unseen pose; 3) Unseen body with unseen pose. We show two examples for each setting. The details are highlighted in the red boxes.} \label{fig:2dvis}

elements.sent-2411-147: We only test the unseen bodies on this dataset and achieve the best performance.
elements.sent-2411-148: We credit this to our more accurate 3D geometry modeling with SDS guidance and SMPL localized features.

elements.sent-2411-150: Compared with GP-NeRF \cite{gpnerf22 , our method can synthesize images with better color and finer details.}
elements.sent-2411-151: As highlighted in the red box of Fig.~\ref{fig:2dvis , GP-NeRF generates wrong colors which are not consistent with the input images for some areas while the colors generated from our method show more consistency.}
elements.sent-2411-152: While our method can synthesize images with more details.
elements.sent-2411-153: This is because with learned tokens as guidance, it can predict the accurate blending weights learned from the training data, which greatly reduces the blurry effect.
elements.sent-2411-154: Also, SDS optimization also helps color prediction since our blending weights prediction is relied on the accurate geometry prediction.
elements.sent-2411-155: We show the ablation studies for 3D reconstruction and novel view synthesis on THuman dataset in terms of learnable tokens, diffusion guidance, multi-target optimization in Tab.\ref{table:ablation .}
elements.sent-2411-156: As shown in the table, we can see that diffusion guidance plays an important role in both 3D reconstruction and novel view synthesis because our whole network is only supervised with images, and the whole network can converge better with the geometric SDS loss as guidance.
elements.sent-2411-157: Our learnable tokens also significantly improve both 3D and 2D results.
elements.sent-2411-158: Learnable tokens provides vertex-to-semantic mapping enables our model to generalize well to unseen subjects.
elements.sent-2411-159: Multi-target training makes the 3D geometry even better both quantitatively and qualitatively.
elements.sent-2411-160: We also show the reconstruction results with different number of input views in Tab.\ref{table:ablation_views .}
elements.sent-2411-161: Increasing the number of input views, both NVS and 3D reconstruction quality are improved.

elements.sent-2411-165: Details in the arms and facial part are highlighted in the red box.
elements.sent-2411-166: Our full model achieves precise 3D reconstruction, capturing fine details with high accuracy.
elements.figure-2411-015: \includegraphics[width=0.48\textwidth]{ablation.pdf} \caption{Ablation studies on each component in our proposed method. See the detailed comparison highlighted in the red boxes.} \label{fig:ablation}
elements.sent-2411-167: In this paper, we introduce DiHuR for generalizable 3D human reconstruction and novel view synthesis from sparse cameras.
elements.sent-2411-168: To mitigate the minimal overlapping view problem, we introduce learnable tokens attached on human parametric model to guide the sparse view feature aggregation and SDF prediction process.
elements.sent-2411-169: Without 3D supervision, we utilize a 2D pre-trained diffusion model as the normal guidance to improve geometry details.
elements.sent-2411-170: We further propose a multi-target training strategy to constrain the underlying 3D surface.
elements.sent-2411-171: Quantitative and qualitative results on the commonly used benchmark show the superiority of our DiHuR compared with existing approaches.. { \bibliographystyle{ieee_fullname.}}
elements.sent-2411-172: If you are submitting a paper to the second round for a paper that received a Revise decision in the first round, please use this template to address the reviewer concerns and highlight the changes made since the original submission.
elements.sent-2411-173: The rebuttal is limited to a {\bf one page PDF file.}
elements.sent-2411-174: Please follow the steps and style guidelines outlined below for submitting your author response.
elements.sent-2411-175: Just like the original submission, the rebuttal must maintain anonymity and cannot include external links that reveal the author identity or circumvent the length restriction.
elements.sent-2411-176: The rebuttal must comply with this template (the use of sections is not required, though it is recommended to structure the rebuttal for ease of reading).
elements.sent-2411-177: Author responses must be no longer than 1 page in length including any references and figures.
elements.sent-2411-178: Overlength responses will simply not be reviewed.
elements.sent-2411-179: This includes responses where the margins and formatting are deemed to have been significantly altered from those laid down by this style guide.
elements.sent-2411-180: Note that this \LaTeX\ guide already sets figure captions and references in a smaller font.
elements.sent-2411-181: All text must be in a two-column format.
elements.sent-2411-182: The total allowable size of the text area is $6\frac78$ inches (17.46 cm) wide by $8\frac78$ inches (22.54 cm) high.
elements.sent-2411-183: Columns are to be $3\frac14$ inches (8.25 cm) wide, with a $\frac{5 {16}$ inch (0.8 cm) space between them.}
elements.sent-2411-184: The top margin should begin 1 inch (2.54 cm) from the top edge of the page.
elements.sent-2411-185: The bottom margin should be $1\frac{1 {8}$ inches (2.86 cm) from the bottom edge of the page for $8.5 \times 11$-inch paper; for A4 paper, approximately $1\frac{5 {8}$ inches (4.13 cm) from the bottom edge of the page.}}
elements.sent-2411-187: It is important for readers to be able to refer to any particular equation.
elements.sent-2411-188: Wherever Times is specified, Times Roman may also be used.
elements.sent-2411-189: Main text should be in 10-point Times, single-spaced.
elements.sent-2411-190: Section headings should be in 10 or 12 point Times.
elements.sent-2411-191: All paragraphs should be indented 1 pica (approx.~$\frac{1 {6}$ inch or 0.422 cm).}
elements.sent-2411-192: Figure and table captions should be 9-point Roman type as in Figure \ref{fig:onecol .}
elements.sent-2411-193: List and number all bibliographical references in 9-point Times, single-spaced, at the end of your response.
elements.sent-2411-194: When referenced in the text, enclose the citation number in square brackets, for example~\cite{Alpher05 .}
elements.sent-2411-195: Where appropriate, include the name(s) of editors of referenced books.

elements.sent-2411-196: To avoid ambiguities, it is best if the numbering for equations, figures, tables, and references in the author response does not overlap with that in the main paper (the reviewer may wonder if you talk about Figure \ref{fig:onecol in the author response or in the paper).}
elements.sent-2411-199: Please ensure that any point you wish to make is resolvable in a printed copy of the response.
elements.sent-2411-200: Resize fonts in figures to match the font in the body text, and choose line widths which render effectively in print.
elements.sent-2411-201: Readers (and reviewers), even of an electronic copy, may choose to print your response in order to read it.
elements.sent-2411-202: You cannot insist that they do otherwise, and therefore must not assume that they can zoom in to see tiny details on a graphic.
